uses: Treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus to control high blood sugar, together with diet and exercise
Used alone or in combination with other diabetes medicines, including insulin
indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control)
Off-label per Drugs.com treatment guides: polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and insulin resistance
common_side_effects: Low blood sugar (mainly when combined with insulin or sulfonylureas)
serious_side_effects: Lactic acidosis (rare, life-threatening): unusual muscle pain, trouble breathing, stomach pain, dizziness, feeling cold, slow or irregular heartbeat, severe weakness or tiredness
Severe hypoglycemia (low blood sugar)
Allergic reaction: hives, difficulty breathing, swelling of face, lips, tongue, or throat
Chest pain
Vitamin B12 deficiency with long-term use

symptom_related_information.symptom: Nausea, vomiting, or stomach pain
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Common gastrointestinal side effects, especially when starting; taking with food helps. However, when paired with unusual muscle pain, weakness, trouble breathing, or feeling cold, they can be early signs of lactic acidosis.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Mild GI upset at the start is common; take with meals. If symptoms are severe, persistent, or occur with muscle pain, weakness, difficulty breathing, or feeling cold, stop and seek emergency care.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Dizziness, lightheadedness, shakiness, sweating, or confusion
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: May indicate low blood sugar (hypoglycemia), more likely when metformin is combined with insulin or a sulfonylurea.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Treat with a fast-acting sugar source; contact a healthcare professional if hypoglycemia is severe or recurrent. Do not change the dose without medical advice.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Diarrhea
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: A common side effect, particularly at the start of treatment or after a dose increase.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Often improves over time and when taken with food; contact a healthcare professional if severe, persistent, or causing dehydration.

warnings: Boxed warning: risk of lactic acidosis, a rare but potentially fatal build-up of lactic acid; risk rises with kidney impairment, age 65+, contrast imaging, surgery, heart failure, liver impairment, and excessive alcohol
Temporarily stop before iodinated-contrast imaging and before surgery; restart only when kidney function is confirmed stable (about 48 hours after)
Not used to treat type 1 diabetes or diabetic ketoacidosis
Kidney function (eGFR) must be checked before and during treatment
May lower vitamin B12 levels with long-term use; periodic monitoring advised
Avoid excessive alcohol; it increases the risk of lactic acidosis and low blood sugar
May restore ovulation and increase the chance of unintended pregnancy in premenopausal women
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (eGFR less than 30 mL/min/1.73 m2)
Acute or chronic metabolic acidosis, including diabetic ketoacidosis
usual_adult_dose: Immediate-release: start 500 mg twice daily or 850 mg once daily; usual maintenance about 2000 mg/day in divided doses; maximum 2550 mg/day. Extended-release: start 500-1000 mg once daily; maximum 2000 mg/day.
usual_adult_frequency: Immediate-release: 2 to 3 times daily with meals. Extended-release: once daily with the evening meal.
meal_relation: Take with meals (with food) to reduce stomach upset; the extended-release form is taken with the evening meal
administration_instructions: Take by mouth with food. Swallow extended-release tablets whole - do not split, crush, or chew. Shake the oral suspension before measuring and use a proper dosing device, not a kitchen spoon. Take at the same time each day; continue even when feeling well and do not stop without medical advice.
missed_dose_instructions: Take the missed dose as soon as you remember. If it is almost time for the next dose, skip the missed dose and continue the regular schedule. Do not take a double dose to make up for a missed one.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature in the original, tightly closed container, away from light, excess heat, and moisture (not in the bathroom). Keep out of reach of children. Extended-release oral suspension: store at 20-25 C (68-77 F) and discard reconstituted suspension after 100 days.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Iodinated contrast dye (used in some X-ray/CT scans)
major_drug_interactions.description: Iodinated contrast can acutely reduce kidney function and decrease metformin clearance.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of metformin-associated lactic acidosis.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (topiramate, acetazolamide, dichlorphenamide, methazolamide, zonisamide)
major_drug_interactions.description: These agents can cause metabolic acidosis, which adds to metformin's lactic acidosis risk.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of lactic acidosis.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Insulin and insulin secretagogues (e.g., sulfonylureas such as glipizide, glyburide)
major_drug_interactions.description: Additive blood-sugar-lowering effect when combined with metformin.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of hypoglycemia; a lower dose of insulin or secretagogue may be needed.

drug_food_interactions.description: Excessive or binge alcohol use increases the risk of lactic acidosis and can cause low blood sugar; limit alcohol and ask your doctor what amount is safe.

drug_disease_interactions.description: Contraindicated at eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73 m2; reduced clearance raises lactic acidosis risk.
Contraindicated; metformin can worsen the acid-base disturbance.
Increase the risk of lactic acidosis.
Not recommended; increases lactic acidosis risk.

monitoring_requirements: Kidney function (eGFR) at baseline and at least annually (more often if at risk)
Blood glucose and HbA1c for glycemic control
Periodic vitamin B12 levels, especially with peripheral neuropathy or anemia
Hematologic parameters annually
patient_counseling_points: Take with meals to reduce stomach upset; GI symptoms often ease over time.
Learn the symptoms of lactic acidosis (muscle pain, trouble breathing, unusual tiredness, feeling cold) and seek emergency care if they occur.
Tell your care team before any surgery or imaging with contrast dye - metformin may need to be paused.
Continue taking even if you feel well; do not stop without consulting your doctor.
Limit alcohol.
Empty tablet shells from the extended-release form may appear in the stool; this is normal.
caregiver_notes: Help the person take metformin with meals at consistent times, watch for warning signs of lactic acidosis and low blood sugar, keep lab appointments for kidney function and vitamin B12, and make sure clinicians are told before any surgery or contrast imaging. Do not adjust the dose without medical advice.

uses: Treatment of high blood pressure (hypertension) in adults and children 6 years and older, alone or with other medicines
Treatment of heart failure (in combination with other medicines)
Improving survival after a heart attack (acute myocardial infarction)
indications: Off-label per Drugs.com: diabetic nephropathy (not an FDA-approved indication)
serious_side_effects: Angioedema: swelling of the face, throat, tongue, lips, eyes, hands, feet, ankles, or lower legs; hoarseness; difficulty breathing or swallowing (medical emergency)
High blood potassium (hyperkalemia): nausea, weakness, tingling, chest pain, irregular heartbeat, loss of movement
Low white blood cell count: fever, sore throat, chills, mouth or skin sores, signs of infection
Kidney problems: swelling, urinating less, tiredness, shortness of breath
Liver problems / jaundice: yellowing of the skin or eyes, dark urine, upper-right stomach pain
Severe low blood pressure: lightheadedness, fainting
Allergic reaction: severe stomach pain, hives, difficulty breathing

symptom_related_information.symptom: Dry, tickly, persistent cough
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: A common and well-recognized side effect of ACE inhibitors like lisinopril; usually not dangerous but can be bothersome.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Not an emergency; tell your doctor if it is troublesome, as safe alternatives (e.g., ARBs) may be considered. Do not stop the medicine on your own.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Swelling of the face, lips, tongue, or throat, or difficulty breathing/swallowing
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: May indicate angioedema, a serious allergic-type reaction to ACE inhibitors.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Seek emergency medical care immediately and stop the medication.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Dizziness or lightheadedness, especially on standing up
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: May reflect low blood pressure from lisinopril, more likely with dehydration, diuretics, or the first doses.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Rise slowly, stay adequately hydrated; contact a healthcare professional if you faint or symptoms persist. Do not change the dose without advice.

warnings: Boxed warning: fetal toxicity - can cause injury or death to a developing fetus; stop immediately and tell your doctor if pregnancy occurs
Risk of angioedema (serious swelling); higher in people with a prior history of angioedema
Do not take within 36 hours before or after a medicine containing sacubitril (e.g., Entresto) due to angioedema risk
If you have diabetes, do not use with aliskiren-containing medicines
Can raise blood potassium; avoid potassium supplements and potassium-containing salt substitutes unless directed
Dehydration (from vomiting, diarrhea, heavy sweating) can cause a large drop in blood pressure, electrolyte imbalance, or kidney failure
Tell surgeons/dentists you take lisinopril before procedures
contraindications: History of angioedema (including ACE-inhibitor-associated or hereditary/idiopathic angioedema)
Hypersensitivity to lisinopril or other ACE inhibitors
Concomitant use with aliskiren in patients with diabetes
usual_adult_dose: Hypertension: start 10 mg once daily (5 mg if on a diuretic); maintenance 20-40 mg once daily; maximum 80 mg/day. Heart failure: start 2.5-5 mg once daily, titrate as tolerated, maximum 40 mg/day. After heart attack: 5 mg, then per protocol, maintenance 10 mg once daily.
usual_adult_frequency: Once daily, at the same time each day
meal_relation: May be taken with or without food; take at the same time each day
administration_instructions: Take by mouth once a day, with or without food, at the same time each day. Measure the oral solution with the supplied device, not a kitchen spoon. Continue taking even if you feel well; do not stop without talking to your doctor. Blood pressure and blood tests will be monitored.
missed_dose_instructions: Take the missed dose as soon as you remember. If it is almost time for the next dose, skip the missed dose and continue on the regular schedule. Do not take a double dose to make up for a missed one.
storage_instructions: Store tightly closed at room temperature, away from excess heat and moisture (not in the bathroom). Do not freeze. Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin, celecoxib, diclofenac, indomethacin, meloxicam, and others)
major_drug_interactions.description: NSAIDs can blunt lisinopril's blood-pressure-lowering effect and, combined with an ACE inhibitor (especially with a diuretic), can impair kidney function and raise potassium.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced blood pressure control and increased risk of kidney injury and hyperkalemia.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Potassium-sparing diuretics, potassium supplements, or potassium-containing salt substitutes (spironolactone, triamterene, amiloride)
major_drug_interactions.description: Additive potassium retention with an ACE inhibitor.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Risk of dangerously high blood potassium (hyperkalemia).

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Sacubitril (e.g., Entresto)
major_drug_interactions.description: Combined ACE inhibition and neprilysin inhibition markedly increase angioedema risk; the two must be separated by at least 36 hours.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Serious risk of angioedema; combination is contraindicated.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Aliskiren (in patients with diabetes or kidney disease)
major_drug_interactions.description: Dual blockade of the renin-angiotensin system.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of kidney impairment, hyperkalemia, and low blood pressure; avoid the combination.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus, temsirolimus)
major_drug_interactions.description: Can increase the risk of angioedema when combined with ACE inhibitors.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased angioedema risk.

drug_food_interactions.food_or_beverage: Potassium-containing salt substitutes and high-potassium supplements
drug_food_interactions.description: Can add to the potassium-raising effect of lisinopril; do not use unless your doctor directs.
May add to the blood-pressure-lowering effect and increase dizziness; use alcohol only in moderation.

drug_disease_interactions.description: Requires dose adjustment and monitoring; lisinopril can worsen kidney function, especially with dehydration or NSAIDs.
Contraindicated; high risk of recurrent serious swelling.
Do not combine with aliskiren; monitor potassium and kidney function.

drug_disease_interactions.disease_or_condition: Volume or salt depletion / low blood pressure
drug_disease_interactions.description: Increased risk of symptomatic hypotension, especially at the first dose; correct before starting.
Stop and seek care if jaundice or marked liver-enzyme elevations occur.

monitoring_requirements: Blood pressure regularly
Kidney function (serum creatinine) and serum potassium, especially after starting or dose changes
White blood cell count in selected patients (e.g., collagen vascular disease or kidney impairment)
Liver enzymes if jaundice or hepatic symptoms occur
patient_counseling_points: A dry cough is common; it is usually harmless but tell your doctor if it bothers you.
Seek emergency help for any swelling of the face, lips, tongue, or throat, or trouble breathing.
Stand up slowly to avoid dizziness, and stay hydrated; report fainting.
Do not use potassium supplements or salt substitutes unless your doctor approves.
Do not stop lisinopril suddenly; high blood pressure often has no symptoms.
Tell your doctor right away if you become or plan to become pregnant.
caregiver_notes: Help ensure once-daily dosing at a consistent time, watch for warning signs of angioedema (facial or throat swelling, trouble breathing) and act as an emergency, monitor for dizziness/fainting and dehydration, avoid potassium salt substitutes, and keep blood-pressure and lab follow-up appointments. Report any pregnancy immediately. Do not change the dose without medical advice.

uses: Lowers LDL ('bad') cholesterol and triglycerides and raises HDL ('good') cholesterol
Reduces the risk of heart attack, stroke, angina, and the need for heart procedures in people with or at risk of heart disease
Used together with diet, exercise, and weight control
indications: Heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia in adults and children 10 years and older
Homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (as an adjunct to other LDL-lowering therapy)
Cardiovascular risk reduction in adults with clinical coronary heart disease or multiple risk factors (including type 2 diabetes)
common_side_effects: Muscle, joint, back, or limb pain
Stuffy or runny nose, sore throat (cold-like symptoms)
Forgetfulness, memory loss, or confusion (reported, usually reversible)
serious_side_effects: Myopathy / rhabdomyolysis: unexplained muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness, especially with fever, unusual tiredness, or dark urine (can lead to kidney failure)
Immune-mediated muscle weakness in hips, shoulders, neck, and back; trouble lifting arms or climbing
Liver problems: loss of appetite, upper-right stomach pain, dark urine, clay-colored stools, jaundice (yellowing of skin or eyes)
Kidney problems: swelling, urinating less, tiredness, shortness of breath
Increased blood sugar (increased thirst/urination, dry mouth, fruity breath)
Serious allergic or skin reaction: hives, trouble breathing, facial/throat swelling, blistering or peeling rash

symptom_related_information.symptom: Unexplained muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness (especially with dark urine or fever)
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: May indicate statin-related myopathy or rhabdomyolysis, an uncommon but serious breakdown of muscle tissue that can harm the kidneys.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Contact your doctor promptly; if severe or with dark urine and fever, seek urgent medical care. Do not stop on your own unless advised.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Yellowing of the skin or eyes, dark urine, or upper-right abdominal pain
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: May signal liver injury, an uncommon serious side effect of atorvastatin.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Contact a healthcare professional; liver tests may be needed.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Forgetfulness, confusion, or memory loss
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Has been reported with statins and is usually reversible after stopping.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Discuss with your doctor; this is generally not an emergency, but do not change therapy without advice.

warnings: Do not use if you have active liver disease or cirrhosis
Can cause muscle breakdown (rhabdomyolysis) leading to kidney failure; risk is higher in older adults and people with kidney disease, hypothyroidism, or certain interacting drugs
May harm a developing fetus; stop and call your doctor if you become pregnant
Do not breastfeed while taking atorvastatin
Drinking more than 2 alcoholic drinks daily increases the risk of liver damage
Limit grapefruit; avoid drinking more than 1.2 liters of grapefruit juice per day
usual_adult_dose: Start 10-20 mg once daily (40 mg once daily if greater than 45% LDL-C reduction is needed); usual range 10-80 mg once daily.
usual_adult_frequency: Once daily, at any consistent time of day
meal_relation: Tablet: with or without food. Oral suspension: on an empty stomach (at least 1 hour before or 2 hours after a meal). Avoid large amounts of grapefruit/grapefruit juice.
administration_instructions: Take the tablet by mouth once daily, with or without food, at the same time each day. Take the oral suspension on an empty stomach; shake it and measure with the supplied device, not a kitchen spoon. Continue taking even if you feel well; do not stop or change the dose without your doctor's advice.
missed_dose_instructions: Take the missed dose as soon as you remember. Skip it if it is almost time for the next dose (within about 12 hours). Never take two doses at once.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature in the original, tightly closed container, away from moisture, heat, and light (not in the bathroom). Keep out of reach of children. Discard any unused oral suspension 60 days after opening the bottle.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, erythromycin, itraconazole, ketoconazole, posaconazole, voriconazole, HIV/hepatitis C protease inhibitors)
major_drug_interactions.description: These raise atorvastatin blood levels by blocking its metabolism.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of muscle toxicity (myopathy and rhabdomyolysis); dose limits or avoidance may be required.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Fibrates (gemfibrozil, fenofibrate) and niacin
major_drug_interactions.description: Combining other lipid-lowering agents with a statin adds muscle-related risk.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Cyclosporine and other organ-transplant rejection medicines
major_drug_interactions.description: Markedly increase atorvastatin exposure.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: High risk of muscle toxicity; combination generally avoided or dose-limited.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Colchicine
major_drug_interactions.description: Additive muscle toxicity when combined with statins.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of myopathy.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Rifampin
major_drug_interactions.description: Can lower atorvastatin levels (and timing of administration matters).
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced cholesterol-lowering effect.

drug_food_interactions.description: Large amounts (more than ~1.2 liters/day of juice) raise atorvastatin levels and the risk of side effects; limit grapefruit products.
Regularly drinking more than 2 drinks per day increases the risk of liver damage.
Reduce the medicine's effectiveness; follow a cholesterol-lowering diet.

drug_disease_interactions.description: Contraindicated in active liver disease; statins can affect liver enzymes.
Higher risk of muscle breakdown (rhabdomyolysis).
Poorly controlled thyroid increases the risk of muscle toxicity.
Statins may modestly raise blood sugar; benefits usually outweigh this risk.

monitoring_requirements: Lipid panel (LDL-C, HDL-C, triglycerides, total cholesterol) about 4 weeks after starting or changing dose, then periodically
Liver enzymes at baseline and if symptoms of liver problems occur
Creatine kinase (CK) if unexplained muscle pain or weakness develops
Blood glucose/HbA1c, especially in people at risk of diabetes
patient_counseling_points: Report any unexplained muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness - especially with dark urine or fever - to your doctor.
Take it at the same time each day; it works whether taken morning or night.
Avoid large amounts of grapefruit and limit alcohol.
Keep taking it even if you feel well; cholesterol usually has no symptoms.
Follow a low-fat, low-cholesterol diet for full benefit.
Stop and call your doctor right away if you become pregnant.
caregiver_notes: Support once-daily dosing at a consistent time, watch for and promptly report muscle pain/weakness (especially with dark urine), and signs of liver trouble such as jaundice. Discourage grapefruit and excess alcohol, encourage the cholesterol-lowering diet, and keep lipid and liver lab appointments. Do not change the dose without medical advice.

uses: Treatment of high blood pressure (hypertension) in adults and children 6 years and older, alone or with other medicines
Treatment of certain types of angina (chest pain), including chronic stable and vasospastic angina
Coronary artery disease - to reduce the risk of hospitalization for angina and the need for a coronary procedure
indications: Coronary artery disease (in patients without heart failure or low ejection fraction)
common_side_effects: Swelling of the ankles, feet, or lower legs (peripheral edema)
serious_side_effects: Worsening chest pain or, rarely, a heart attack when first starting or increasing the dose (chest pain/pressure, pain spreading to jaw or shoulder, nausea, sweating)
Very low blood pressure or fainting
Rapid, pounding, or irregular heartbeat
Allergic reaction: hives, difficulty breathing, swelling of the face, lips, tongue, or throat

symptom_related_information.symptom: Swelling of the ankles, feet, or lower legs
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: A common, dose-related side effect of amlodipine (peripheral edema) caused by its effect on small blood vessels; it does not usually respond to water pills (diuretics).
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Tell your doctor; they may adjust the dose or change the blood-pressure medicine. Not usually an emergency unless accompanied by breathlessness.

symptom_related_information.symptom: New or worsening chest pain, especially when starting or after a dose increase
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Rarely, amlodipine can worsen angina or, very rarely, trigger a heart attack at the start of treatment or dose increase.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Seek emergency medical attention for severe, ongoing chest pain, pressure spreading to the jaw/shoulder, sweating, or nausea.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Dizziness or lightheadedness
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Often due to amlodipine lowering blood pressure, especially when standing up quickly.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Rise slowly from sitting or lying; contact a healthcare professional if you feel faint or pass out.

warnings: Chest pain (angina) may worsen, or rarely a heart attack may occur, when first starting amlodipine or when the dose is increased
Use caution with severe narrowing of the aortic heart valve (aortic stenosis) and in liver disease (lower starting doses may be needed)
Alcohol can further lower blood pressure and increase some side effects
Keep taking it even if you feel well; high blood pressure often has no symptoms
Rise slowly to avoid dizziness from low blood pressure
available_strengths: 2.5 mg, 5 mg, 10 mg (single-dose powder for oral solution)
usual_adult_dose: Hypertension: start 5 mg once daily (2.5 mg in older, frail, or small adults and those with liver impairment); maximum 10 mg/day. Angina / coronary artery disease: 5-10 mg once daily; maximum 10 mg/day.
usual_adult_frequency: Once daily, at the same time each day
meal_relation: May be taken with or without food; take at the same time each day
administration_instructions: Take by mouth once daily, with or without food, at a consistent time. Shake the oral suspension and measure liquid forms with the supplied device, not a kitchen spoon. The Sdamlo powder must be mixed with water and taken within 60 minutes. Continue taking even if you feel well; do not stop or change the dose without your doctor's advice.
missed_dose_instructions: Take the missed dose as soon as you remember. If it is less than 12 hours until the next dose, skip the missed dose and continue your regular schedule. Do not take a double dose to make up for a missed one.
storage_instructions: Store tablets and oral solution at room temperature away from light, excess heat, and moisture (not in the bathroom). Store the oral suspension in the refrigerator and do not freeze; protect it from light. Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Simvastatin (and lovastatin)
major_drug_interactions.description: Amlodipine and these statins are both processed by the CYP3A4 enzyme; amlodipine raises simvastatin blood levels.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of statin muscle toxicity (myopathy/rhabdomyolysis); simvastatin is generally limited to a maximum of 20 mg/day when taken with amlodipine.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, itraconazole, ketoconazole, ritonavir, and similar)
major_drug_interactions.description: These reduce the breakdown of amlodipine, raising its blood levels.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased blood-pressure-lowering effect, dizziness, and swelling; closer monitoring may be needed.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Other blood-pressure-lowering medicines (other antihypertensives, nitrates)
major_drug_interactions.description: Additive lowering of blood pressure when combined with amlodipine.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of low blood pressure, dizziness, or fainting.

drug_food_interactions.description: Grapefruit inhibits the CYP3A4 enzyme that breaks down amlodipine and can modestly increase its levels; many clinicians advise limiting grapefruit.
Can further lower blood pressure and increase dizziness or other side effects.

drug_disease_interactions.description: Vasodilation may worsen symptoms; use with caution.
Use with caution; tell your doctor if you have congestive heart failure.
Amlodipine is cleared more slowly; lower starting doses are recommended.
Rarely, angina or a heart attack can worsen at the start of treatment or with dose increases.

monitoring_requirements: Blood pressure, checked regularly
Heart rate and any chest-pain pattern
Watch for swelling (peripheral edema)
Liver function in patients with hepatic impairment
patient_counseling_points: Ankle/leg swelling is a common side effect; report it rather than treating it yourself.
Stand up slowly to reduce dizziness.
Keep taking it even if you feel well; blood pressure often has no symptoms.
Take it at the same time each day, with or without food.
Report any worsening or new chest pain, particularly soon after starting or increasing the dose.
Limit alcohol, which can lower your blood pressure further.
caregiver_notes: Support once-daily dosing at a consistent time, watch for ankle/leg swelling and report it, help the person rise slowly to avoid dizziness, and treat any severe or new chest pain as an emergency. Keep blood-pressure follow-up appointments and do not change the dose without medical advice.

uses: Treatment of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and frequent heartburn
Healing of erosive esophagitis and prevention of further esophageal damage
Treatment of stomach (gastric) and duodenal ulcers, including those caused by H. pylori (used with antibiotics)
Treatment of conditions with excess stomach acid such as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
Over-the-counter: short-term control of frequent heartburn (2 or more days per week)
indications: H. pylori eradication (in combination with antibiotics)
common_side_effects: Cold-like symptoms (stuffy nose, sore throat) and fever, especially in children
In men, difficulty achieving or maintaining an erection
serious_side_effects: Severe watery or bloody diarrhea with stomach pain or fever (possible Clostridioides difficile infection)
Kidney problems: less urination, blood in urine, fever, rash, joint pain, nausea, loss of appetite
Low magnesium: dizziness, irregular heartbeat, jitteriness, muscle cramps or spasms, seizures
Bone fractures of the hip, wrist, or spine (with long-term or high-dose use)
New or worsening lupus: joint pain and a sun-sensitive rash on the cheeks or arms
Severe skin reactions: blistering, peeling, or bleeding skin; mouth/genital sores
Serious allergic reaction: rash, hives, swelling of the face/lips/tongue/throat, trouble breathing
Vitamin B12 deficiency with use longer than 3 years; stomach (fundic gland) polyps with long-term use

symptom_related_information.symptom: Watery or bloody diarrhea with severe stomach pain or fever
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: PPIs like omeprazole can increase the risk of a serious intestinal infection (C. difficile); persistent diarrhea may signal this.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Contact your doctor before taking any anti-diarrhea medicine; seek care if severe or with fever.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Dizziness, irregular heartbeat, muscle cramps or spasms, jitteriness

symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Contact a healthcare professional; magnesium levels may need to be checked.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Chest pain spreading to the jaw or shoulder with sweating or lightheadedness
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Heartburn can mimic a heart attack; this is not a typical omeprazole side effect and may be a cardiac emergency.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Seek emergency medical help immediately.

warnings: Not intended for immediate relief of heartburn; it may take 1 to 4 days to work
Heartburn can mimic a heart attack - get emergency help for chest pain spreading to the jaw or shoulder with sweating or lightheadedness
Can cause kidney problems; report reduced urination or blood in the urine
Long-term or more-than-once-daily use increases the risk of bone fractures
Can cause low magnesium and, after more than 3 years, vitamin B12 deficiency
May cause or worsen lupus (joint pain with a sun-sensitive rash)
OTC use: do not take for more than 14 days or repeat more often than every 4 months without medical advice
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to omeprazole or other proton pump inhibitors (e.g., esomeprazole, lansoprazole, pantoprazole, rabeprazole)
Concomitant use with rilpivirine-containing HIV medicines
dosage_forms: Delayed-release granules / powder for oral suspension
usual_adult_dose: Over-the-counter: 20 mg once daily for 14 days. Prescription: usually taken once daily before a meal; taken twice daily when combined with antibiotics for H. pylori; and up to three times daily before meals for conditions with excess stomach acid. Available as 10, 20, and 40 mg.
usual_adult_frequency: Usually once daily before a meal (often in the morning); twice daily for H. pylori therapy; up to three times daily for acid-hypersecretory conditions
meal_relation: Take before a meal (OTC: in the morning, at least 1 hour before eating); taking it with food reduces how much is absorbed
administration_instructions: Swallow delayed-release capsules and tablets whole with water; do not split, chew, or crush them. If swallowing is hard, a capsule may be opened and the granules sprinkled on a tablespoon of cool applesauce and swallowed immediately without chewing. Mix granules/powder for suspension with water as directed and drink promptly. Take OTC omeprazole for only 14 days unless advised otherwise.
missed_dose_instructions: Take the missed dose as soon as you remember. If it is almost time for the next dose, skip the missed dose and continue your regular schedule. Do not take a double dose to make up for a missed one.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature in the original, tightly closed container, away from light, excess heat, and moisture (not in the bathroom). Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Clopidogrel
major_drug_interactions.description: Omeprazole can reduce the activation of clopidogrel (a blood thinner) by inhibiting the CYP2C19 enzyme.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced antiplatelet effect, potentially increasing clot risk; an alternative acid reducer is often preferred.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Rilpivirine-containing HIV medicines (e.g., Complera, Odefsey, Juluca)
major_drug_interactions.description: Reduced stomach acid markedly lowers rilpivirine absorption.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Loss of HIV treatment effectiveness; the combination is contraindicated.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Methotrexate (especially high-dose)
major_drug_interactions.description: Omeprazole can raise methotrexate blood levels.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of methotrexate toxicity; a temporary PPI break may be advised.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Digoxin
major_drug_interactions.description: Reduced stomach acid can increase digoxin absorption.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Higher digoxin levels; monitoring may be needed.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Drugs that need stomach acid to be absorbed (certain azole antifungals such as ketoconazole/itraconazole, atazanavir, iron salts, levothyroxine)
major_drug_interactions.description: Omeprazole reduces stomach acid, lowering absorption of these medicines.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced effectiveness of the affected medicine; dose or monitoring changes may be needed.

drug_food_interactions.description: There are no specific foods to avoid, but taking omeprazole with food reduces its absorption; take it before a meal (about 1 hour before eating).

drug_disease_interactions.description: Long-term or high-dose use increases the risk of fractures.
May affect omeprazole handling; tell your doctor before use.
PPIs can trigger or worsen lupus symptoms.

drug_disease_interactions.disease_or_condition: Low magnesium, calcium, or potassium / hypoparathyroidism
drug_disease_interactions.description: Omeprazole can lower magnesium; caution and monitoring may be needed.
Risk of acute kidney injury (interstitial nephritis); report reduced urination or blood in urine.

monitoring_requirements: Magnesium levels with long-term use
Vitamin B12 with use longer than 3 years
Kidney function if symptoms of kidney problems occur
Bone health in people at risk of fracture
Symptom response - reassess if symptoms do not improve or recur
patient_counseling_points: Take it before a meal (often morning, about 1 hour before eating); it is not for instant relief and may take a few days to work.
Swallow capsules/tablets whole; if needed, sprinkle capsule granules on applesauce and swallow right away.
For OTC use, take for only 14 days and do not repeat more often than every 4 months without medical advice.
Report watery or bloody diarrhea, severe stomach pain, or signs of low magnesium (muscle cramps, irregular heartbeat).
Seek emergency care for chest pain spreading to the jaw or shoulder with sweating - heartburn can mimic a heart attack.
Discuss long-term risks (bone fractures, low magnesium, vitamin B12) if you take it for a long time.
caregiver_notes: Help give omeprazole before meals and ensure capsules/tablets are swallowed whole (or granules given on applesauce). Watch for persistent watery/bloody diarrhea, signs of low magnesium, and reduced urination, and treat chest pain spreading to the jaw or shoulder as an emergency. For OTC use, keep courses to 14 days and discuss long-term use with a doctor. Do not change therapy without medical advice.

Reduces fever
Relieves pain, swelling, and stiffness of osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis
indications: Mild to moderate pain, including menstrual (period) pain
Other inflammatory arthritis such as ankylosing spondylitis, gouty arthritis, and psoriatic arthritis
serious_side_effects: Heart attack or stroke: chest pain spreading to the jaw or shoulder, sudden weakness or numbness on one side, slurred speech, shortness of breath
Stomach or intestinal bleeding: bloody or black/tarry stools, vomit that is bloody or looks like coffee grounds, severe stomach pain
Kidney problems: little or no urination, swelling of the feet/ankles, tiredness or shortness of breath
Liver problems: nausea, upper-right stomach pain, dark urine, jaundice (yellow skin or eyes)
Serious allergic reaction or severe skin reaction (hives, facial/throat swelling, blistering/peeling rash)
Fluid retention, high blood pressure, or heart failure (swelling, rapid weight gain, breathlessness)
Anemia (pale skin, lightheadedness, rapid heartbeat); rarely aseptic meningitis (headache, stiff neck, fever)

symptom_related_information.symptom: Black or tarry stools, bloody or coffee-ground vomit, or severe stomach pain
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Signs of stomach or intestinal bleeding, a serious NSAID risk that can occur without warning.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Stop taking ibuprofen and seek urgent medical care.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Chest pain, shortness of breath, weakness on one side of the body, or slurred speech
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: NSAIDs can increase the risk of heart attack and stroke.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Call emergency services immediately.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Swelling of the feet, ankles, or legs, or sudden weight gain
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: May reflect fluid retention or effects on the kidneys or heart from ibuprofen.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Contact a healthcare professional; do not continue without advice if this develops.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Ringing in the ears or dizziness
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Common, usually mild NSAID side effects.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: If persistent or bothersome, stop and ask a healthcare professional; consider the lowest effective dose.

warnings: Boxed warning: increased risk of serious, potentially fatal heart attack and stroke; do not use just before or after heart bypass (CABG) surgery
Boxed warning: risk of serious, potentially fatal stomach/intestinal ulcers and bleeding, especially in older adults, with long use, or with alcohol
Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time needed
Avoid during the last 20 weeks of pregnancy unless directed by a doctor (risk to the baby's heart and kidneys)
Use caution with heart disease, high blood pressure, heart failure, kidney or liver disease, and asthma
Do not combine with other NSAIDs or other pain products containing the same type of ingredient
contraindications: History of asthma attack, hives, or severe allergic reaction after aspirin or other NSAIDs
In the setting of coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery
usual_adult_dose: Over-the-counter: 200-400 mg every 4-6 hours as needed; do not exceed 1200 mg/day (no more than 6 doses of 200 mg in 24 hours) unless directed. Prescription: maximum 800 mg per dose and 3200 mg per day.
usual_adult_frequency: Every 4 to 6 hours as needed for pain or fever; 3 to 4 times daily for arthritis. Use the lowest effective dose.
meal_relation: Take with food or milk to reduce stomach upset (low OTC doses may be taken on an empty stomach for faster relief)
administration_instructions: Take by mouth with water; swallow tablets whole and chew chewable tablets before swallowing. Shake the liquid and measure with the supplied device, not a kitchen spoon. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time. Do not take other products containing ibuprofen or other NSAIDs at the same time.
missed_dose_instructions: Ibuprofen is often taken only as needed. If you take it on a schedule, take the missed dose when you remember, but skip it if it is almost time for the next dose. Never take two doses at once.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature, away from excess heat and moisture (not in the bathroom). Do not allow liquid forms to freeze. Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.description: Ibuprofen can block aspirin's antiplatelet effect if taken too close together.

major_drug_interactions.description: Additive effects on bleeding plus NSAID stomach irritation.

major_drug_interactions.description: Combining with an NSAID increases bleeding tendency.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Lithium and methotrexate
major_drug_interactions.description: Ibuprofen can raise blood levels of these drugs.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of lithium or methotrexate toxicity.

drug_food_interactions.description: Increases the risk of stomach bleeding; avoid or limit alcohol while taking ibuprofen.

drug_disease_interactions.disease_or_condition: Heart disease, high blood pressure, or heart failure
drug_disease_interactions.description: NSAIDs raise the risk of cardiovascular events and can cause fluid retention and worsen blood pressure/heart failure.

drug_disease_interactions.disease_or_condition: History of stomach ulcer or GI bleeding
drug_disease_interactions.description: Higher risk of serious ulcers and bleeding.
NSAIDs can reduce kidney function, especially with dehydration or other kidney-affecting drugs.
Can trigger severe bronchospasm in aspirin/NSAID-sensitive asthma.
Use with caution; rare liver injury can occur.

monitoring_requirements: Blood pressure
Kidney function, especially with long-term use or in at-risk people
Signs of stomach bleeding (black stools, coffee-ground vomit)
Complete blood count (for anemia) with prolonged use
Liver enzymes if symptoms of liver problems occur
patient_counseling_points: Use the lowest dose that works, for the shortest time; do not exceed label/prescribed limits.
Take with food or milk if it upsets your stomach.
Stop and seek care for black/tarry stools, bloody or coffee-ground vomit, or severe stomach pain.
Seek emergency help for chest pain, breathlessness, or stroke symptoms.

Avoid in the last 20 weeks of pregnancy unless your doctor advises, and limit alcohol.
caregiver_notes: Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time, give with food if it causes stomach upset, and avoid doubling up with other NSAID-containing products. Watch for signs of stomach bleeding (black stools, coffee-ground vomit) and treat chest pain or stroke symptoms as an emergency. Be especially cautious in older adults and people with heart, kidney, or ulcer history. For children, dose strictly by weight/age per the package and use the supplied measuring device.

Do not take more than 4000 mg per day from all sources, and do not take more than one product containing acetaminophen (also labeled APAP) at a time
Do not use if you drink 3 or more alcoholic drinks per day without talking to your doctor
Do not use with severe liver disease
If an overdose is suspected, get medical help right away even if you feel well

meal_relation: May be taken with or without food

Always check labels so you do not take more than one acetaminophen-containing product.
missed_dose_instructions: Acetaminophen is usually taken as needed. If you are on a regular schedule, take the missed dose when you remember unless it is almost time for the next dose, then skip it. Do not take a double dose.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature in the original, tightly closed container, away from excess heat and moisture (not in the bathroom). Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Warfarin (and other coumarin anticoagulants)

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Other acetaminophen-containing products (many cold/flu, pain, and sleep combinations)
major_drug_interactions.description: Taking more than one product that contains acetaminophen adds up.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Accidental overdose and serious liver damage.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Alcohol

drug_food_interactions.description: Increases the risk of liver damage; avoid or limit alcohol, and do not use acetaminophen if you have 3 or more alcoholic drinks per day without medical advice.

drug_disease_interactions.description: Contraindicated in severe liver disease; lower daily limits may be needed in other liver conditions.
Higher risk of liver toxicity even at usual doses.
Some chewable forms contain aspartame (a source of phenylalanine); check the label.

monitoring_requirements: Track total daily acetaminophen from all products (stay at or below 4000 mg/day)
Liver function in people with liver disease, heavy alcohol use, or long-term high-dose use
INR if taken regularly with warfarin

patient_counseling_points: Never exceed 4000 mg in 24 hours, and check every medicine label for acetaminophen or APAP.
Do not take two acetaminophen-containing products at the same time.
Avoid alcohol; the combination can harm the liver.
If you think you took too much, get help immediately even if you feel fine.

Talk to your doctor about using acetaminophen during pregnancy.
caregiver_notes: Keep a running total of acetaminophen from every product, since many cold/flu, pain, and sleep medicines contain it (often shown as APAP) - exceeding the daily limit can cause serious liver damage. For children, dose strictly by weight/age and use only the measuring device that came with that product. If an overdose is even possible, seek emergency care right away, before symptoms appear.

symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: NSAIDs can increase the risk of heart attack and stroke.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Call emergency services immediately.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Swelling of the ankles, feet, or legs, or sudden weight gain
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: May reflect fluid retention or effects on the kidneys or heart.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Contact a healthcare professional and do not continue without advice.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Ringing in the ears
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: A common NSAID side effect.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: If persistent or bothersome, stop and seek advice; consider the lowest effective dose.

warnings: Boxed warning: increased risk of serious, potentially fatal heart attack and stroke; do not use just before or after heart bypass (CABG) surgery
Boxed warning: risk of serious, potentially fatal stomach/intestinal ulcers and bleeding, especially in older adults, with long use, or with alcohol
Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time
Avoid during the last 20 weeks of pregnancy unless directed by a doctor
Delayed-release and extended-release forms are for chronic conditions and do not work fast enough for acute pain
Use caution with heart disease, high blood pressure, heart failure, kidney disease, and asthma; may temporarily reduce female fertility
contraindications: History of asthma attack, hives, or severe allergic reaction after aspirin or other NSAIDs
In the setting of coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery
usual_adult_dose: Over-the-counter: 220 mg every 8-12 hours as needed (follow label limits). Prescription: 250-500 mg twice daily for arthritis; every 6-8 hours as needed for other pain; every 8 hours for acute gout. Extended-release forms are taken once daily. Use the lowest effective dose.
usual_adult_frequency: Twice daily for arthritis; every 6-8 hours as needed for pain; once daily for extended-release; OTC every 8-12 hours as needed
meal_relation: Take with food or milk (and a full glass of water) to reduce nausea/stomach upset; ask before using antacids, as some can reduce naproxen absorption
administration_instructions: Take by mouth with water; take with food or milk if it upsets your stomach. Swallow delayed-release and extended-release tablets whole - do not split, chew, or crush. Shake the suspension and measure with the supplied device. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time, and do not take other NSAID-containing products at the same time.
missed_dose_instructions: If taken as needed, no schedule applies. If you take it on a schedule, take the missed dose when you remember unless it is almost time for the next dose, then skip it. Do not take a double dose.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature in the original, tightly closed container, away from excess heat, moisture, and light (not in the bathroom). Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Anticoagulants / blood thinners (warfarin) and antiplatelet drugs
major_drug_interactions.description: Naproxen adds to bleeding effects and irritates the stomach lining; it can raise bleeding risk markedly in people on blood thinners.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Significantly increased risk of serious bleeding.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: SSRIs/SNRIs (antidepressants such as fluoxetine, sertraline, duloxetine, venlafaxine)
major_drug_interactions.description: Combining with an NSAID increases bleeding tendency.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Easier bruising and increased risk of stomach bleeding.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Other NSAIDs, aspirin, or salicylates
major_drug_interactions.description: Adds to GI and kidney toxicity; naproxen may also interfere with low-dose aspirin's heart-protective effect.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Higher risk of bleeding/ulcers and possible loss of aspirin cardioprotection.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone)
major_drug_interactions.description: Additive risk of stomach ulcers and bleeding.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of GI ulceration and bleeding.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Lithium, methotrexate, and pemetrexed
major_drug_interactions.description: Naproxen can raise blood levels of these drugs.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of lithium, methotrexate, or pemetrexed toxicity.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Blood-pressure medicines (ACE inhibitors/ARBs) plus diuretics
major_drug_interactions.description: NSAIDs can reduce their effect and, with a diuretic, can harm the kidneys.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced blood-pressure control and risk of kidney injury.

drug_food_interactions.description: Increases the risk of stomach bleeding; avoid or limit alcohol while taking naproxen.
Taking naproxen with food or milk reduces stomach upset.

drug_disease_interactions.disease_or_condition: Heart disease, high blood pressure, or heart failure
drug_disease_interactions.description: NSAIDs raise cardiovascular risk and can cause fluid retention and worsen blood pressure/heart failure.

drug_disease_interactions.disease_or_condition: History of stomach ulcer or GI bleeding
drug_disease_interactions.description: Higher risk of serious ulcers and bleeding.
Naproxen can reduce kidney function, especially with dehydration or other kidney-affecting drugs.
Can trigger severe bronchospasm in aspirin/NSAID-sensitive asthma.
Use with caution; rare liver injury can occur.

monitoring_requirements: Blood pressure
Kidney function, especially with long-term use or in at-risk people
Signs of stomach bleeding (black stools, coffee-ground vomit)
Complete blood count (for anemia) with prolonged use
Liver enzymes if symptoms of liver problems occur
patient_counseling_points: Use the lowest dose that works, for the shortest time; do not exceed label/prescribed limits.
Take with food or milk and a full glass of water to reduce stomach upset.
Stop and seek care for black/tarry stools, bloody or coffee-ground vomit, or severe stomach pain.
Seek emergency help for chest pain, breathlessness, or stroke symptoms.
Do not combine with other NSAIDs; ask before combining with low-dose aspirin.
Avoid in the last 20 weeks of pregnancy unless your doctor advises, and limit alcohol.
caregiver_notes: Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time, give with food or milk if it causes stomach upset, and avoid combining with other NSAID-containing products. Watch for signs of stomach bleeding (black stools, coffee-ground vomit) and treat chest pain or stroke symptoms as an emergency. Be especially cautious in older adults and people with heart, kidney, or ulcer history. Remember that delayed/extended-release forms are not for fast pain relief.

uses: Relief of allergy and cold symptoms: sneezing, runny nose, itchy/watery eyes, itching of the nose or throat, hives, and skin rash
Short-term treatment of insomnia in adults (sleep aid)
Prevention and treatment of motion sickness
Management of certain Parkinson's disease symptoms and as part of treating allergic reactions
indications: Allergic rhinitis and other allergy symptoms
common_side_effects: Next-day grogginess or 'hangover' feeling after nighttime use
Excitement or agitation (especially in children, sometimes in older adults)
serious_side_effects: Pounding or fluttering heartbeat
Painful or difficult urination, or little/no urination (urinary retention)
Confusion or feeling like you might pass out
Tightness in the neck or jaw, or uncontrollable tongue movements
Serious allergic reaction: hives, difficulty breathing, swelling of the face/lips/tongue/throat
Anticholinergic toxicity in overdose (which can be fatal)

symptom_related_information.symptom: Strong drowsiness, grogginess, dizziness, or loss of coordination
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Expected sedating effects of this first-generation antihistamine; they can persist into the next day after nighttime use.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Do not drive or operate machinery; avoid alcohol. If excessive or persistent, reduce use and ask a healthcare professional.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Difficulty or painful urination, or passing little/no urine
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Anticholinergic effect of diphenhydramine, more likely in men with an enlarged prostate.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Stop and contact your doctor.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Confusion, hallucinations, pounding heartbeat, or feeling faint
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Can reflect anticholinergic toxicity or overdose, more likely with high doses or in older adults.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Seek medical care; for suspected overdose call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or emergency services.

warnings: Causes drowsiness - do not drive or operate machinery until you know how it affects you
Alcohol and other sedating medicines add to the drowsiness
Do not use to make a child sleepy, and do not give to a child under 2 years (misuse of cough/cold medicines can be fatal in very young children)
Generally should be avoided in older adults (except for serious allergic reactions) due to confusion, falls, urinary retention, and other anticholinergic risks
Use caution with glaucoma, enlarged prostate/urinary problems, asthma or COPD, heart disease, and thyroid disorder
Can affect the results of allergy skin tests
contraindications: Use to sedate a child / in children under 2 years without medical advice
usual_adult_dose: 25-50 mg every 4-6 hours as needed for allergy/cold symptoms; do not exceed about 300 mg in 24 hours. As a sleep aid: 50 mg about 30 minutes before bedtime. For motion sickness: take about 30 minutes before travel.
usual_adult_frequency: Every 4 to 6 hours as needed for allergy/cold symptoms; once at bedtime when used as a sleep aid
meal_relation: May be taken with or without food. For motion sickness, take about 30 minutes before travel, then with meals and at bedtime as needed.
administration_instructions: Take by mouth as directed on the label. Measure liquid with a proper dosing device, not a household spoon. Use only for a short time until symptoms clear. As a sleep aid, take within 30 minutes of bedtime; for motion sickness, take 30 minutes before travel. Do not take other antihistamine-containing products at the same time.
missed_dose_instructions: Diphenhydramine is usually taken as needed. If you are on a schedule, take the missed dose when you remember unless it is almost time for the next dose, then skip it. Do not take a double dose.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature in the original, tightly closed container, away from excess heat and moisture (not in the bathroom). Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: CNS depressants (alcohol, opioid pain medicines, sedatives/sleeping pills, benzodiazepines, muscle relaxers, and medicines for anxiety, depression, or seizures)
major_drug_interactions.description: Add to diphenhydramine's sedating and breathing-slowing effects.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Excessive drowsiness, impaired coordination, and in severe cases slowed breathing.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Other antihistamines or anticholinergic medicines (including many cold, allergy, and sleep combination products, tricyclic antidepressants, bladder/Parkinson anticholinergics)
major_drug_interactions.description: Add to anticholinergic effects and can lead to too much total antihistamine.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision, and confusion - especially in older adults.

drug_food_interactions.description: Increases drowsiness and dizziness; avoid alcohol while taking diphenhydramine.

drug_disease_interactions.description: Anticholinergic effects can worsen glaucoma; use with caution.
Can cause or worsen difficulty urinating.

drug_disease_interactions.disease_or_condition: Asthma, COPD, or chronic breathing problems
drug_disease_interactions.description: Can thicken secretions and should be used cautiously.
Use with caution; rarely affects heart rhythm or blood pressure.
Older adults are more sensitive to confusion, falls, urinary retention, and other anticholinergic effects; generally avoid except for serious allergic reactions.

monitoring_requirements: Degree of sedation and impact on driving/alertness
In older adults: watch for confusion, falls, and urinary retention
Reassess if symptoms last more than 7 days or occur with fever, headache, cough, or rash
patient_counseling_points: Expect drowsiness; do not drive or operate machinery and avoid alcohol.
Use only short-term; see a doctor if symptoms last more than 7 days or come with a fever and headache, cough, or rash.
Do not give to children under 2 and never use it to make a child sleepy.
Older adults should generally use safer alternatives; ask a doctor or pharmacist.
Do not combine with other antihistamine-containing cold, allergy, or sleep products.
For sleep, take about 30 minutes before bed; for motion sickness, 30 minutes before travel.
caregiver_notes: Do not use diphenhydramine to make a child sleepy and never give it to a child under 2 years. Be cautious in older adults, who are sensitive to confusion, falls, urinary retention, and daytime drowsiness - safer alternatives often exist. Avoid combining with other antihistamine-containing products and with alcohol or other sedating medicines, and ensure the person does not drive while drowsy.

uses: Lowers the risk of stroke and blood clots in people with atrial fibrillation not caused by a heart valve problem
Prevents blood clots (DVT/PE) after hip or knee replacement surgery
Treats deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE) and reduces the risk of them returning
indications: VTE prophylaxis after hip or knee replacement
Venous thromboembolism in children (after initial anticoagulation)
common_side_effects: Bleeding that takes longer than usual to stop
In children: headache, vomiting, heavy menstrual bleeding
serious_side_effects: Severe or life-threatening bleeding: blood in urine, bloody/tarry stools, coughing up blood, coffee-ground vomit, heavy vaginal bleeding, any bleeding that won't stop
Spinal or epidural blood clot (with spinal procedures): back pain, leg numbness/weakness, loss of bladder or bowel control - can cause paralysis
Higher risk of stroke or clot if the medicine is stopped suddenly
Serious allergic reaction: hives, chest tightness, wheezing, swelling of face/lips/tongue/throat

symptom_related_information.symptom: Bleeding that will not stop, blood in urine or stool, or coffee-ground vomit
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Apixaban is a blood thinner; bleeding is its main serious risk.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Seek emergency medical help immediately.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Back pain, leg numbness or weakness, or loss of bladder/bowel control after a spinal injection or tap
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: May indicate a spinal/epidural blood clot, a rare but serious complication.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Get emergency medical help right away.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Sudden numbness, weakness, confusion, or trouble speaking
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Could be a stroke/clot, more likely if doses are missed or the drug is stopped.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Call emergency services immediately; do not stop apixaban on your own.

warnings: Increases the risk of serious or fatal bleeding, especially with other blood-affecting medicines
Do not stop taking it without your doctor's advice - stopping raises the risk of stroke or clots
Usually stopped 24-48 hours before surgery or dental/medical procedures (longer if kidney problems) - follow your doctor's plan
Risk of spinal/epidural blood clots with spinal taps or epidurals
Not for people with mechanical (artificial) heart valves or triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome
Tell all doctors and dentists you take apixaban; report any fall or head injury
usual_adult_dose: Atrial fibrillation: 5 mg twice daily (2.5 mg twice daily if at least two of: age 80+, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 1.5 mg/dL or higher). DVT/PE treatment: 10 mg twice daily for 7 days, then 5 mg twice daily; 2.5 mg twice daily for long-term recurrence prevention. Hip/knee replacement prophylaxis: 2.5 mg twice daily.
meal_relation: May be taken with or without food
administration_instructions: Take by mouth twice daily as prescribed. If you cannot swallow tablets, 2.5 mg and 5 mg tablets may be crushed and mixed in water, D5W, apple juice, or applesauce and taken promptly. Do not run out or stop without medical advice, and report any falls or injuries (especially head injuries).
missed_dose_instructions: Take the missed dose as soon as you remember on the same day, then return to the twice-daily schedule. Do not take two doses at once to make up for a missed dose.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature, 20-25 C (68-77 F). Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Other anticoagulants/antiplatelets, NSAIDs, aspirin, SSRIs/SNRIs, heparin, warfarin, clopidogrel
major_drug_interactions.description: Add to the bleeding effect.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Significantly increased risk of serious bleeding.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Combined P-glycoprotein and strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir)
major_drug_interactions.description: Raise apixaban blood levels.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased bleeding risk; the dose is reduced by half (or the combination avoided at the 2.5 mg dose).

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Strong dual inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, St. John's wort)
major_drug_interactions.description: Lower apixaban blood levels.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced protection against clots.

drug_food_interactions.description: Can raise apixaban levels and bleeding risk; limit them. (Unlike warfarin, apixaban is not affected by vitamin K / leafy greens.)
Increases the risk of stomach bleeding and falls/injury.

drug_disease_interactions.description: Affects dosing and bleeding risk; dose and timing around procedures may change.
Can increase bleeding risk; use with caution.
Higher risk of serious bleeding.
Apixaban is not effective/appropriate in these conditions.

monitoring_requirements: Signs of bleeding or bruising
Kidney and liver function periodically
No routine INR monitoring is required (unlike warfarin)
patient_counseling_points: Take it twice every day; do not stop without your doctor's advice - stopping raises stroke/clot risk.
Seek emergency care for bleeding that won't stop, blood in urine/stool, or coughing up blood.
Tell all providers and dentists you take apixaban before any procedure.
Avoid grapefruit/pomelo/Seville orange and limit alcohol; you do NOT need to avoid leafy greens.
Report any fall or head injury promptly.
Do not run out - refill before your supply ends.
caregiver_notes: Ensure the twice-daily doses are taken and never stopped abruptly, since stopping increases stroke/clot risk. Watch for bleeding (bruising, blood in urine/stool, coughing blood) and treat uncontrolled bleeding as an emergency. Report falls or head injuries, coordinate stopping before procedures with the prescriber, and keep apixaban refilled. No INR testing is needed.

Do not stop without talking to your doctor - stopping raises the risk of heart attack or stroke
May work less well in people with certain genetic differences in how they process it (CYP2C19)
Avoid the acid reducers omeprazole and esomeprazole, which can lower its effectiveness
Tell surgeons/dentists you take clopidogrel; it may be paused before procedures

administration_instructions: Take by mouth once daily, with or without food. If your doctor has prescribed aspirin with it, take the aspirin only as directed. Do not stop without medical advice, and tell any healthcare provider treating you that you take clopidogrel.
missed_dose_instructions: Take the missed dose as soon as you remember, but skip it if it is almost time for the next dose. Do not take two doses at once.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature away from moisture and heat. Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced antiplatelet effect; an alternative such as pantoprazole or famotidine is preferred if an acid reducer is needed.

major_drug_interactions.description: Increase bleeding tendency.

Can affect activation and bleeding risk.
Use with caution and monitoring.

Seek care for bleeding that won't stop, blood in urine/stool, or coughing up blood.
Avoid omeprazole/esomeprazole; ask about pantoprazole or famotidine if you need an acid reducer.
Tell surgeons and dentists you take clopidogrel before any procedure.
Take aspirin with it only if your doctor tells you to.
Limit alcohol to reduce stomach-bleeding risk.

uses: Treatment of fluid retention (edema) in heart failure, liver disease, or kidney disorders (including nephrotic syndrome)
Treatment of high blood pressure (hypertension)
Rapid treatment of acute pulmonary edema (by injection)
serious_side_effects: Dehydration and electrolyte imbalance: muscle cramps, weakness, irregular heartbeat, severe thirst, confusion
Ringing in the ears or hearing loss (can be permanent with high or rapid IV doses)
Severe low blood pressure or fainting
Kidney problems; signs of liver or pancreas problems (severe stomach pain, jaundice)
High blood sugar; low potassium/magnesium
Serious allergic or skin reaction; easy bruising or unusual bleeding

symptom_related_information.symptom: Ringing in the ears or hearing changes
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Furosemide can affect hearing, especially at high doses or rapid IV administration.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Contact your doctor promptly; this can sometimes be permanent.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Muscle cramps, weakness, or irregular heartbeat
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: May indicate low potassium, sodium, or magnesium from the strong diuretic effect.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Contact your doctor; electrolytes may need checking and replacing.

symptom_related_information.symptom: Severe dizziness, fainting, very dry mouth, or little urination
symptom_related_information.possible_relation_to_drug: Signs of dehydration or very low blood pressure.
symptom_related_information.recommended_action: Seek medical care and follow your doctor's fluid advice.

warnings: Do not use if you are unable to urinate
High doses can cause irreversible hearing loss
Strong diuresis can cause water and electrolyte depletion - careful medical supervision and monitoring are needed
Tell your doctor about sulfa-drug allergy
Contrast dye plus furosemide can harm the kidneys
Increases sun sensitivity - use sun protection; keep taking it for blood pressure even if you feel well
contraindications: Hepatic coma or severe electrolyte depletion until corrected
usual_adult_dose: Edema: 20-80 mg by mouth once, repeated or increased by 20-40 mg every 6-8 hours until the desired effect, then the effective dose once or twice daily. Hypertension: about 40 mg twice daily. Acute pulmonary edema: 40 mg IV (may repeat).
usual_adult_frequency: Once or twice daily for most oral uses; often taken earlier in the day to avoid nighttime urination
meal_relation: May be taken with or without food; take earlier in the day to limit nighttime urination
administration_instructions: Take by mouth as prescribed, measuring liquid with a proper device. Do not take more than prescribed (high doses risk hearing loss). missed_dose_instructions: If on a regular schedule, take the missed dose when you remember, but skip it if it is almost time for the next dose. Do not take two doses at once.
storage_instructions: Store at room temperature away from light, moisture, and heat (oral solution may need refrigeration per the label). Keep out of reach of children.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Lithium
major_drug_interactions.description: Furosemide reduces lithium clearance, raising its levels.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of lithium toxicity.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Ototoxic/nephrotoxic drugs (aminoglycoside antibiotics, cisplatin)
major_drug_interactions.description: Additive damage to hearing and kidneys.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin, and others)
major_drug_interactions.description: NSAIDs blunt the diuretic effect and stress the kidneys.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced effectiveness and risk of kidney injury.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Sucralfate
major_drug_interactions.description: Sucralfate reduces furosemide absorption if taken together.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced furosemide effect; separate doses by at least 2 hours.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Other blood-pressure medicines
major_drug_interactions.description: Additive blood-pressure lowering.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased risk of low blood pressure and dizziness.

drug_food_interactions.description: Can increase dizziness and the blood-pressure-lowering effect.
Furosemide lowers potassium and sodium; follow your doctor's advice on potassium-rich foods or supplements and salt intake.

drug_disease_interactions.description: Dose and response vary; monitor kidney function and electrolytes.
Electrolyte shifts can precipitate hepatic coma; use with caution.
Raises uric acid and can trigger gout.
Can raise blood sugar.
Increased urine output can worsen urinary retention symptoms.

monitoring_requirements: Electrolytes (potassium, sodium, magnesium)
Kidney function

major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Markedly increased risk of serious bleeding.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Many antibiotics and antifungals (e.g., metronidazole, fluconazole, ciprofloxacin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole)
major_drug_interactions.description: Can raise warfarin's effect and the INR.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Increased bleeding risk; closer INR monitoring is needed.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Amiodarone and certain other heart/CYP-interacting drugs
major_drug_interactions.description: Increase warfarin levels/effect.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Higher INR and bleeding risk; dose reduction and monitoring needed.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Vitamin K supplements and enzyme inducers (e.g., rifampin, St. John's wort)
major_drug_interactions.description: Reduce warfarin's effect.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Lower INR and increased clot risk.

major_drug_interactions.interacting_drug: Herbal products (ginkgo, garlic, ginseng, cranberry, coenzyme Q10, echinacea, goldenseal)
major_drug_interactions.description: Can raise or lower the INR or add to bleeding risk.
major_drug_interactions.clinical_effect: Unpredictable changes in anticoagulation.

drug_food_interactions.food_or_beverage: Vitamin K-rich foods (leafy green vegetables, liver, some vegetable oils)
drug_food_interactions.description: Make warfarin less effective; do not avoid them entirely - keep your weekly intake consistent.

drug_food_interactions.food_or_beverage: Grapefruit, cranberry, pomegranate, and noni juices
drug_food_interactions.description: Can increase warfarin's effect and bleeding risk; avoid these juices.
Can change the INR and increase bleeding risk; avoid or strictly limit.

drug_disease_interactions.description: Greatly increased bleeding risk; warfarin is generally avoided.
Alters clotting and warfarin metabolism; needs careful monitoring.
Can affect dosing and bleeding risk.
Increases the risk of serious bleeding (e.g., brain hemorrhage).

monitoring_requirements: INR / prothrombin time regularly (target usually 2.0-3.0, condition-dependent)
Signs of bleeding or bruising
Review of new medicines, supplements, illnesses, and diet changes that affect INR
patient_counseling_points: Take it at the same time daily and never double up; keep every INR appointment.
Seek emergency care for bleeding that won't stop, blood in urine/stool, or coughing up blood.
Do not start or stop any medicine, supplement, or herbal without checking - many change your INR.
Keep vitamin K foods (leafy greens) consistent rather than avoiding them; avoid cranberry/grapefruit juice and alcohol.
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin for pain unless your doctor approves; use acetaminophen-based options as advised.
Wear a medical-alert ID and tell all providers (including dentists) you take warfarin.
caregiver_notes: Help ensure the exact dose is taken at the same time daily and that INR blood tests are never missed. Watch closely for bleeding (bruises, blood in urine/stool, coughing blood) and treat uncontrolled bleeding as an emergency. Keep diet's vitamin K consistent, avoid cranberry/grapefruit juice and alcohol, prevent injuries, and check with the pharmacist before any new medicine or supplement. Coordinate stopping warfarin before surgeries/procedures with the prescriber.

when_user_reports_symptoms: The assistant should identify whether the symptom is a known medication side effect.
The assistant should mention possible medication-related causes only when supported by sources.
The assistant should advise contacting a healthcare professional for serious or concerning symptoms.
when_user_asks_how_to_take_medication: Provide administration instructions.
Provide number of doses per day when available.
Include meal timing recommendations when available.
